title: High Energy Bills or Cleaner Air? Poland’s Struggle Over Green Energy
content:
The latest data reveal a distinctly Polish paradox: although we breathe air that is damaging our health, fears over household finances and a deep distrust of institutions have made Poles some of Europe’s biggest sceptics of the green transition.

We breathe air that, in many European capitals, would trigger a public health alert. The latest report by the European Environment Agency (EEA), published in April 2026, paints a troubling picture of Poland as one of the continent’s most polluted countries. Yet, according to recent opinion polls, Poles remain among the least enthusiastic supporters of a rapid shift toward clean energy.

Why, despite having so much to gain, are we still so reluctant to embrace change? To understand this contradiction, we need to look at the hard data, the realities of household budgets, and the way climate policy is experienced in everyday life.

What are we actually breathing — and how does Poland compare to Europe?

Before turning to the broader political debate, it is wo

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Czech Panic Over Alleged EU Ban on ‘Dvanáctka’ Beer Labels Proves False
content:
In mid-May, the European Commission’s representation in Prague found itself issuing an unusual reassurance on social media: “Brussels does not want to ban the twelve-degree beer.”

Out of context, the statement may have sounded absurd. But it was a direct response to headlines circulating in parts of the Czech media claiming that the European Union planned to abolish the traditional Czech system of beer labelling – including iconic terms such as “desítka” and “dvanáctka,” referring to the beer’s degree of bitterness and density. As with many viral EU-related claims, the reports turned out to have little basis in reality.

In one of Europe’s most secular societies, few things are sacred. Beer, however, comes close.

And so when reports emerged suggesting the EU wanted to outlaw the traditional Czech “dvanáctka,” the reaction was immediate.

Several Czech outlets published stories claiming the EU was preparing rules to standardise beer labelling across member states. Under the alleged proposal, beers would reportedly need to be labelled solely according to alcohol content rather than the traditional Cz

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Dan Jørgensen: The Current Crisis Is the Worst in History
content:
The European Union is facing the worst energy crisis in its history, but it is far better prepared today than it was in 2022, EU Commissioner for Energy and Housing Dan Jørgensen told FocusEurope.pl.

The Strait of Hormuz remains blocked, while Brent crude prices have hit record highs in recent days. On May 6, the benchmark climbed above $114 per barrel — the highest level since the start of the U.S.-Israeli operation in Iran.

“We are facing the worst energy crisis in history. Many say the current crisis is worse than the crises of 1973 and 2022 combined,” Dan Jørgensen told journalists, including FocusEurope.pl, on Monday (May 5).

According to the commissioner, the European Union is currently spending around €500 million per day to cope with the consequences of the crisis.

“That is a clear sign of Europe’s structural weakness,” he stressed.

At the same time, Jørgensen argued that the situation differs significantly from that of 2022. The EU, he said, is now much better equipped to withstand the crisis thanks to the expansion of renewable energy, a stronger focus on energy efficiency, an
snippet: The European Union is facing the worst energy crisis in its history, but it is far better prepared today than it was in 2022, EU Commissioner for Energy and Housing Dan Jørgensen told FocusEurope.pl.
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Europe Trapped in an Energy Dilemma
content:
In 2022, the European Union launched the REPowerEU programme, setting itself the goal of ending dependence on Russian fossil fuels by 2027. In 2025, the European Commission published a detailed roadmap outlining the bloc’s complete phase-out of Russian energy imports.

In January 2026, EU member states formally adopted legislation requiring the phase-out of Russian gas imports by autumn 2027. Yet despite this, Russia continues to break records in LNG exports to Europe.

More than four years after the outbreak of the war in Ukraine, Russian liquefied natural gas is still flowing into European ports in unprecedented volumes. A continent that once declared its energy emancipation from Moscow continues to pay Russia billions of euros every year — no longer for pipeline gas, but for LNG.

The war in Ukraine did not end Europe’s financing of the Russian energy sector; it merely changed its form. At the same time, the crisis in the Middle East and the sharp increase in gas imports from the United States reveal that the European Union may simply be replacing one dependency with another.

In the first quarter of 2026, Europe
snippet: In 2022, the European Union launched the REPowerEU programme, setting itself the goal of ending dependence on Russian fossil fuels by 2027. In 2025, the European Commission published a detailed roadma
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Finns among top nations for spreading wind power misinformation in Europe
content:
According to Wind Europe, Finland ranks fourth in Europe for the spread of falsehoods about wind energy. However, opposition to wind power is also deeply tied to local politics and genuine community concerns. 

Misinformation is delaying or even preventing wind power projects in Europe, claims Wind Europe in a new report. As a Brussels-based advocacy organisation representing the interests of wind power companies, its views are not impartial.

However, several independent researchers have also reached a similar conclusion: that false information is being disseminated on a large scale and often in a coordinated manner against renewable energy. According to a research report published last year, Russian trolls have also been campaigning against renewable energy on social media. 

According to a Wind E

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: MEPs call for freezing Slovakia’s EU funds for the second time – and issue a “last warning”
content:
The European Parliament has escalated pressure on Slovakia for the second time in a short period. While the appeal is not binding on the Commission, the pressure on Brussels to act is mounting.

On Wednesday afternoon, MEPs approved another resolution criticising developments in the country under Robert Fico’s fourth government.

While the first resolution three weeks ago focused on the misuse of European funds, the latest text was broader in scope. MEPs expressed deep concern over the state of democracy, the rule of law and respect for fundamental rights in Slovakia.

A majority of the 537 MEPs

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Tribunal for Putin’s war is taking shape – Slovakia still hesitates to join
content:
Despite repeatedly stressing the importance of international law, Robert Fico’s government still has not signed the convention establishing the Special Tribunal for the Crime of Aggression against Ukraine, citing “unresolved administrative and operational issues.” A legal expert says the explanation is unconvincing.

The EU has backed the creation of a tribunal modelled on the Nuremberg trials that would prosecute Russian leaders for aggression against Ukraine. On 5 May, ministers from member states voted for the bloc to formally join the court established under the Council of Europe, three European officials confirmed to The Kyiv Independent.

According to the platform United24, which operates under the auspices of the Ukrainian government, the EU will also become a founding member of the tribunal.

The European Commission had already

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: B9 Summit Pushes “NATO 3.0” as Alliance Looks Toward Ankara
content:
The concept of “NATO 3.0”, introduced in the joint declaration of the Bucharest 9 and Nordic countries summit, could also be included in the final declaration of the Alliance summit in Ankara this June.

NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte said this on Wednesday during a joint press conference with Romanian President Nicușor Dan and Polish President Karol Nawrocki following the B9 meeting in Bucharest.

“NATO 3.0” envisions a stronger Europe within a stronger Alliance.

The joint declaration adopted by the B 9 states and the Nordic countries – whose leaders met in Bucharest on Wednesday in the presence of NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy, and U.S. Deputy Under Secretary of State Thomas DiNanno – is the first official high-level document to endorse the concept of ”NATO 3.0”.

The

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: European Stability Mechanism in the Face of Geopolitical and Structural Challenges
content:
During the latest Eurogroup meeting, ministers and EU officials presented a comprehensive assessment of the challenges facing the European financial sector. 

The discussion focused on growing macroeconomic risks linked to the conflict in the Middle East, the resilience of the banking system and the need for greater cross-border banking consolidation within the EU.

Pierre Gramegna, Managing Director of the European Stability Mechanism (ESM), placed the current architecture of European financial markets within the troubling macroeconomic context created by the crisis in the Persian Gulf.

“In recent weeks, market reactions appeared excessively optimistic. Despite declarations about peace efforts, the Strait of Hormuz has not reopened and oil prices have started rising again,” Gramegna said. He also warned against short-sightedness even in the event of a rapid end to the conflict:

“We know that even if the conflict were resolved in the short term, its economic consequences would last much longer than the conflict itself.”

In his assessment, the possibility of a major market correction

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Europe’s Labour Divide: 13 Million Unemployed as Poland Defies the Trend
content:
Europe’s labour market is catching its breath after years of overlapping crises, yet EU statistics continue to reveal a vast divide between double-digit unemployment rates in the south of the continent and record-high employment levels in Poland.

When European statistical offices compiled labour market data for March 2026, the picture emerging from the latest Eurostat reports brought a degree of reassurance — but also raised difficult questions about the future. The European Union, still grappling with the legacy of multiple crises over the past decade, appears to be standing on relatively stable, though deeply uneven, ground.

While the overall unemployment rate across the EU remained at 6 per cent, behind these seemingly reassuring figures lies a labour force of more than 13 million people still searching for work.

Spring 2026 has so far been marked by stabilisation. In March, the number of unemployed people across the European Union stood at exactly 13.226 million. The figure was almost identical to the level recorded a
